subject: Physics
q: The motion of a simple pendulum executing S.H.M. is represented by the following equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, where time is measured in second. The length of pendulum is
question: The motion of a simple pendulum executing S.H.M. is represented by the following equation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>π</mi><mi>t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi>ϕ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, where time is measured in second. The length of pendulum is
solution:
Comparing the given equation y=Asinπt+ϕ with a standard equation y=Asinωt+ϕ. We have

ω=π rad s-1

Since time period is  T=2πω=2 s, it is a second's pendulum.

T=2πLg⇒L=gπ2=99.4 cm

subject: Physics
q: A particle executes S.H.M. of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the position of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and it moves along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The displacement of particle in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>δ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>δ</mi></math> will be
question: A particle executes S.H.M. of amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> along <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. At <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math>, the position of the particle is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>A</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> and it moves along positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis. The displacement of particle in time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi><mo> </mo><mi>sin</mi><mfenced><mrow><mi>ω</mi><mi>t</mi><mo> </mo><mo>+</mo><mo> </mo><mi>δ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>, then the value <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>δ</mi></math> will be
solution:
Given, the instantaneous equation of motion of the particle is

x=A sinωt+δ   ...1

As the particle is moving along positive x-axis, v=dxdt>0.

Substitute 0 for t and A2 for x into equation (1) and solve to calculate the required phase difference.

A2=Asinω×0+δ⇒sinδ=12=sinπ6⇒δ=π6

q: In the given figure, a mass M is attached to a horizontal spring which is fixed on one side to a rigid support. The spring constant of the spring is k. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period T and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, as shown in the figure, another mass m is gently fixed upon it. question: In the given figure, a mass M is attached to a horizontal spring which is fixed on one side to a rigid support. The spring constant of the spring is k. The mass oscillates on a frictionless surface with time period T and amplitude A. When the mass is in equilibrium position, as shown in the figure, another mass m is gently fixed upon it. The new amplitude of oscillation will be:
solution:
Momentum of system remains conserved.

pi=pf

MAω=m+MA'ω

MAkM=m+MA'km+M

A'=AMM+m

q: In a simple harmonic oscillator, at the mean position
question: In a simple harmonic oscillator, at the mean position

q: A block of mass m attached to a massless spring is performing oscillatory motion of amplitude 'A' on a frictionless horizontal plane. If half of the mass of the block breaks off when it is passing through its equilibrium point, the amplitude of oscillation for the remaining system become ƒA. The value of ƒ is:
question: A block of mass m attached to a massless spring is performing oscillatory motion of amplitude 'A' on a frictionless horizontal plane. If half of the mass of the block breaks off when it is passing through its equilibrium point, the amplitude of oscillation for the remaining system become ƒA. The value of ƒ is:
solution:
Using mometum conservation 

m1v1=m2v2mAω=m2A2ω2mAkm=m2A2km/2A2=2A⇒f=2

subject: Physics
q: A simple harmonic oscillator has an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>π</mi></math> second. Assuming the oscillation starts from its mean position, the time required by it to travel from<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>A</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
question: A simple harmonic oscillator has an amplitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mi>π</mi></math> second. Assuming the oscillation starts from its mean position, the time required by it to travel from<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>A</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><msqrt><mn>3</mn></msqrt><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>A</mi></math> will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi>x</mi></mfrac><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mi>_</mi><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
From phasor diagram particle has to move from P to Q in a circle of radius equal to amplitude of SHM.

cosϕ=3 A2 A=32

⇒ϕ=π6

Now, π6=ωt

⇒π6=2πTt

⇒π6=2π6πt

⇒t=π2

So, x=2

subject: Physics
q:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
question:
In a linear Simple Harmonic Motion (SHM)

(A) Restoring force is directly proportional to the displacement.

(B) The acceleration and displacement are opposite in direction.

(C) The velocity is maximum at mean position.

(D) The acceleration is minimum at extreme points.

Choose the correct answer from the options given below:
solution:
The equations of SHM is given by

F=–kx

⇒a=–ω2x

Velocity is given by, v=ωA2-x2.

Clearly, velocity is maximum at mean position and acceleration is maximum at extreme positions.

Hence, this is the correct option.

subject: Physics
q:
A particle is subjected two simple harmonic motions as :
$\mathrm{x}_1=\sqrt{7} \sin 5 \mathrm{tcm}$
and $x_2=2 \sqrt{7} \sin \left(5 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}$
where x is displacement and $t$ is time in seconds. The maximum acceleration of the particle is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. The value of x is :
question:
A particle is subjected two simple harmonic motions as :
$\mathrm{x}_1=\sqrt{7} \sin 5 \mathrm{tcm}$
and $x_2=2 \sqrt{7} \sin \left(5 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right) \mathrm{cm}$
where x is displacement and $t$ is time in seconds. The maximum acceleration of the particle is $\mathrm{x} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. The value of x is :
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{x}_1=\sqrt{7} \sin 5 \mathrm{t} \\ & \mathrm{x}_2=2 \sqrt{7} \sin \left(5 \mathrm{t}+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)\end{aligned}$
From phasor,

$\therefore$ Amplitude of resultant $\mathrm{SHM}=7$
$\begin{aligned} & \phi=\tan ^{-1} \frac{2 \sqrt{7} \times \sqrt{3} / 2}{\sqrt{7}+2 \sqrt{7} \times \frac{1}{2}}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{21}}{2 \sqrt{7}}=\tan ^{-1} \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \\ & \therefore \quad \mathrm{X}_{\mathrm{R}}=7 \sin (5 \mathrm{t}+\phi) \\ & \quad \mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{R}}=-7 \times 25 \sin (5 \mathrm{t}+\phi) \\ & \therefore \mathrm{a}_{\max }=175 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{sec}=175 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{sec}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The potential energy of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> in motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The time period of the particle for small oscillation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>≃</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
question: The potential energy of a particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> in motion along the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-axis is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">U</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>4</mn><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>-</mo><mi>cos</mi><mn>4</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. The time period of the particle for small oscillation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>sin</mi><mi>θ</mi><mo>≃</mo><mi>θ</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">K</mi></mfrac></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
Given here, potential energy, U=41-cos4x J

Using the relation between conservative force and potential energy, F=-dUdx.

We have, F=-4+sin4x4=-16sin4x

For small θ, sinθ≈θ.

Acceleration of particle is a=-64xm=-64x4=-16x

As the oscillations are simple harmonic in nature, so  a=-ω2x⇒ω2=16⇒ω=4 rad s-1

Now, time period of oscillation isT=2πω=π2.

Thus, the value of K=2.

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is oscillating with an angular frequency $10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The support of the pendulum starts oscillating up and down with a small angular frequency of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ and an amplitude of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The relative change in the angular frequency of the pendulum is best given by :
question: A simple pendulum of length $1 \mathrm{~m}$ is oscillating with an angular frequency $10 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The support of the pendulum starts oscillating up and down with a small angular frequency of $1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ and an amplitude of $10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$. The relative change in the angular frequency of the pendulum is best given by :
solution:
Angular frequency of pendulum $\omega=\sqrt{\frac{g}{\ell}}$

$\therefore$ relative change in angular frequency

$\frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega}=\frac{1}{2} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{g}}[$ as length remains constant $]$

$\Delta g=2 \mathrm{~A} \omega_{s}^{2}\left[\omega_{s}=\right.$ angular frequency of support and, $\mathrm{A}=$

amplitude]

$\frac{\Delta \omega}{\omega}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \mathrm{~A} \omega_{\mathrm{S}}^{2}}{\mathrm{~g}}$

$\Delta \omega=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2 \times 1^{2} \times 10^{-2}}{10}=10^{-3} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{sec}$

q: An oscillator of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is at rest in its equilibrium position in a potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mi>X</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> comes from the right with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and collides completely inelastic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and sticks to it. This process repeats every time the oscillator crosses its equilibrium position. The amplitude of oscillations after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn></math> collisions is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: An oscillator of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> is at rest in its equilibrium position in a potential, <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mn>2</mn></mfrac><mi>k</mi><msup><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>x</mi><mi> </mi><mo>–</mo><mi>X</mi></mrow></mfenced><mn>2</mn></msup></math> . A particle of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>m</mi></math> comes from the right with speed <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>u</mi></math> and collides completely inelastic with <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>M</mi></math> and sticks to it. This process repeats every time the oscillator crosses its equilibrium position. The amplitude of oscillations after <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>13</mn></math> collisions is: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>M</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>10</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>m</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>u</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>k</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
In the first collision mu momentum will be imparted to the system. In the second collision when the momentum of (M + m) is in the opposite direction mu momentum of the particle will make its momentum zero. On 13th collision, 


Applying momentum conservation
mu=(M+13m)v

⇒v=muM+13m=u15 ⇒M=2m
⇒v= ωA

⇒u15= KM+13m×A

⇒A=115751=13

q: Two identical springs of spring constant 2k are attached to a block of mass m and to fixed support (see figure). When the mass is displaced from equilibrium position on either side, it executes simple harmonic motion. The time period of oscillations of this system is :
question: Two identical springs of spring constant 2k are attached to a block of mass m and to fixed support (see figure). When the mass is displaced from equilibrium position on either side, it executes simple harmonic motion. The time period of oscillations of this system is :
solution:
For parallel combination keq=k1+k2

keq=4k

T=2πmkeq⇒T=2πm4k⇒T=πmk

34×12Ka2=12 Ka2-y2
y2=a2-3a24
y=a2

subject: Physics
q:
The displacement time graph of a particle executing SHM is given in figure: (sketch is schematic and not to scale) 

Which of the following statements is/are true for this motion? 

(A) The force is zero at t=3T4
(B) The magnitude of acceleration is maximum at t=T
(C) The speed is maximum at t=T4
(D) The P.E. is equal to K.E. of the oscillation at t=T2
question:
The displacement time graph of a particle executing SHM is given in figure: (sketch is schematic and not to scale) 

Which of the following statements is/are true for this motion? 

(A) The force is zero at t=3T4
(B) The magnitude of acceleration is maximum at t=T
(C) The speed is maximum at t=T4
(D) The P.E. is equal to K.E. of the oscillation at t=T2
solution:
From graph equation of SHM.

X=Acosωt
(A) at 3T4 particle at mean position
∴a=0
F=0
(B) at T particle again at extreme position so acceleration is maximum
(C) at t=T4, particle is at mean position so velocity maximum. ∴  a=0
(D) KE=PE
12kA2-x2=12kx2
A2=2x2
x=+A2

A2=Acosωt

t=T8
∴ A, B and C are correct.

subject: Physics
q: A ring is hung on a nail. It can oscillate, without slipping or sliding (i) in its plane with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and (ii) back and forth in a direction perpendicular to its plane, with a period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
question: A ring is hung on a nail. It can oscillate, without slipping or sliding (i) in its plane with a time period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and (ii) back and forth in a direction perpendicular to its plane, with a period <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>T</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> will be :
solution:
The time period of oscillation of physical pendulum is given by

T=2πIMgl  ...(1)

Where, I is the moment of inertia of ring about given axis and l is the distance of centre of mass of ring from the axis of rotation.

Moment of inertia of ring in its plane is given by

I1=Ic+ Md2=MR2+MR2=2MR2

Moment of inertia of ring perpendicular to its plane is given by

I2=Ic+ Md2=MR22+MR2=32MR2

Now the ratio of time period is given by 

T1T2=I1I2

⇒T1T2=2MR232MR2

⇒T1T2=43=23

subject: Physics
q:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-IList-II
A. $\sin^2 \omega t$I. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ but not simple harmonic motion (SHM)
B. $\sin^3(2\omega t)$II. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ but Not SHM
C. $\sin(\omega t) + \cos(\pi \omega t)$III. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ and SHM
D. $\cos\omega t + \cos 2\omega t$IV. Non-periodic

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Match List-I with List-II.
List-IList-II
A. $\sin^2 \omega t$I. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ but not simple harmonic motion (SHM)
B. $\sin^3(2\omega t)$II. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ but Not SHM
C. $\sin(\omega t) + \cos(\pi \omega t)$III. Periodic with time period $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ and SHM
D. $\cos\omega t + \cos 2\omega t$IV. Non-periodic

Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution:
For A: $y = \sin^2 \omega t = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\omega t}{2} = \dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{2} \cos 2\omega t$

This represents simple harmonic motion (SHM) about the mean position $y = \dfrac{1}{2}$ with an angular frequency of $2\omega$. The time period is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, A matches III.

For B: $y = \sin^3(2\omega t) = \dfrac{3\sin 2\omega t - \sin 6\omega t}{4}$

This is a superposition of two SHMs with angular frequencies $2\omega$ and $6\omega$. It is periodic but not SHM. The time period is the LCM of $T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$ and $T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{6\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{3\omega}$, which is $T = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, B matches I.

For C: $y = \sin(\omega t) + \cos(\pi \omega t)$

The angular frequencies are $\omega_1 = \omega$ and $\omega_2 = \pi \omega$. The ratio $\dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{1}{\pi}$ is an irrational number. Therefore, the function is non-periodic. Thus, C matches IV.

For D: $y = \cos \omega t + \cos 2\omega t$

This is a superposition of two SHMs with angular frequencies $\omega$ and $2\omega$. It is periodic but not SHM. The time period is the LCM of $T_1 = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$ and $T_2 = \dfrac{2\pi}{2\omega} = \dfrac{\pi}{\omega}$, which is $T = \dfrac{2\pi}{\omega}$. Thus, D matches II.

The correct matching is A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II.

Answer: A-III, B-I, C-IV, D-II

q: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
question: For a simple harmonic motion in a mass spring system shown, the surface is frictionless. When the mass of the block is 1 kg, the angular frequency is ω1. When the mass block is 2 kg the angular frequency is ω2. The ratio ω2ω1 is :
solution:
In case of SHM in spring-block system, angular frequency is given by, ω=km.

Therefore,

ω2ω1=m1m2=12

subject: Physics
q: The displacement of a particle varies according to the relation $x=4(\cos \pi t+\sin \pi t)$. The amplitude of the particle is
question: The displacement of a particle varies according to the relation $x=4(\cos \pi t+\sin \pi t)$. The amplitude of the particle is
solution:
$\left.x=4(\cos \pi t+\sin \pi t)=4\left[\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi t\right)\right]+\sin \pi t\right]=4\left[2 \times \sin \left(\frac{\pi t-\frac{\pi}{2}-\pi t}{2}\right) \cos \left(\frac{\pi \mathrm{t}-\frac{\pi}{2}+\pi \mathrm{t}}{2}\right)\right]$
$=8\left[\sin \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \cos \left(-\frac{\pi}{4}+\pi t\right)\right]$
$=\frac{8}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \cos \left[\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]=4 \sqrt{2} \cos \left[\pi t-\frac{\pi}{4}\right]$
Comparing it with standard equation
$X=A \cos (w t-K x) \quad \Rightarrow A=4 \sqrt{2}$

f1=12πkm and 

f2=12πk9m

Required ratio, f1f2=91=31

subject: Physics
q: If a spring has time period T, and is cut into n equal parts, then the time period of each part will be
question: If a spring has time period T, and is cut into n equal parts, then the time period of each part will be
solution:
Spring constant becomes $n$ times for each piece. $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{k}}$
$$
\frac{T_1}{T_2}=\frac{\sqrt{n K}}{K} \text { or } T_2=T / \sqrt{n}
$$

subject: Physics
q: The total energy of particle, executing simple harmonic motion is
question: The total energy of particle, executing simple harmonic motion is
solution: In simple harmonic motion, as a particle is displaced from its mean position, its kinetic energy is converted to potential energy and vice versa and total energy remains constant. The total energy of simple harmonic motion is independent of $x$.

The velocity of the body was tripled by air Jet when it is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from its mean position. question: A body is performing simple harmonic with an amplitude of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The velocity of the body was tripled by air Jet when it is at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> from its mean position. The new amplitude of vibration is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msqrt><mi>x</mi></msqrt><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
In SHM, velocity of the particle at any displacement, x is given by, v=A2-x2.

Therefore,

⇒v3v=A12-25A22-25  ⇒A22-25=3×75

⇒A22=700  ⇒A2=700 cm

question: The displacement of a damped harmonic oscillator is given by <math><mi>x</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><msup><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>0.1</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></mrow></msup><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">cos</mi></mrow><mo>⁡</mo><mrow><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>10</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">π</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">φ</mi></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mrow><mo>.</mo></math> Here <math><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi></math> is in seconds. The time taken for its amplitude of vibration to drop to half of its initial value is close to:
solution:
Amplitude A=A0e-kt
From given condition, A02=A0e-0.1×t
⇒ln2=0.1×t
t=ln20.1=0.6930.10=6.935≈7 s

subject: Physics
q: A mass $\mathrm{M}$ is suspended from a spring of negligible mass. The spring is pulled a little and then released so that the mass executes SHM of time period T. If the mass is increased by $\mathrm{m}$, the time period becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}$. Then the ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}$ is
question: A mass $\mathrm{M}$ is suspended from a spring of negligible mass. The spring is pulled a little and then released so that the mass executes SHM of time period T. If the mass is increased by $\mathrm{m}$, the time period becomes $\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}$. Then the ratio of $\frac{\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}$ is
solution:
$\mathrm{T}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}}{\mathrm{K}}}$
$\frac{5 \mathrm{~T}}{3}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{K}}}$
Dividing equation (ii) by equation (i), $\frac{5}{3}=\sqrt{\frac{\mathrm{M}+\mathrm{m}}{\mathrm{M}}}$. Squaring both the sides $\frac{25}{9}=\frac{M+m}{M}=1+\frac{m}{M} \Rightarrow \frac{m}{M}=\frac{25}{9}-1=\frac{16}{9}$

subject: Physics
q: Two bodies of masses 1 kg and 4 kg are connected to a vertical spring, as shown in the figure. The smaller mass executes simple harmonic motion of angular frequency 25 rad s-1, and amplitude 1.6 cm while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. The maximum force exerted by the system on the floor is (take g = 10 m s-2).
question: Two bodies of masses 1 kg and 4 kg are connected to a vertical spring, as shown in the figure. The smaller mass executes simple harmonic motion of angular frequency 25 rad s-1, and amplitude 1.6 cm while the bigger mass remains stationary on the ground. The maximum force exerted by the system on the floor is (take g = 10 m s-2).
options: 20 N
60 N
40 N
10 N
solution:
Given ω = 2 5 rad/s and A = 1.6 cm

∴   ω=km=25 ⇒k= 25 2

Maximum compression in the spring

=xo+A

=mgk+A

∴  Fmax=4g + k(x0 + A)

Fmax =4g+kmgk+A

            = 40 + 20 = 60 N

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of maximum acceleration to maximum velocity in a simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> At, <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> the displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum acceleration? The initial phase is <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> .
question: The ratio of maximum acceleration to maximum velocity in a simple harmonic motion is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mi> </mi><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> At, <math><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> the displacement is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo>.</mo></math> What is the maximum acceleration? The initial phase is <math><mfrac><mrow><mi>π</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac></math> .
solution:
In a simple harmonic motion,

Maximum acceleration, fmax=ω2A

Maximum velocity, vmax=Aω. 

fmaxvmax=ω2AAω=ω

ω=10

Displacement in SHM is x=Asin⁡ωt+π4

At t=0

5=Asin⁡π4

A=52. 

Max acceleration = ω2A

=100×52

=5002  m s-2

subject: Physics
q: A simple pendulum oscillating in air has period <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . The bob of the pendulum is completely immersed in a non-viscous liquid. The density of the liquid is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of the material of the bob. If the bob is inside liquid all the time, its period of oscillation in this liquid is:
question: A simple pendulum oscillating in air has period <math><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi></math> . The bob of the pendulum is completely immersed in a non-viscous liquid. The density of the liquid is <math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>16</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi mathvariant="normal">t</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">h</mi></math> of the material of the bob. If the bob is inside liquid all the time, its period of oscillation in this liquid is:
solution:
Let the density of bob is σ & density of liquid is  σ16 .
Now, Net downward force on bob = Weight - Buoyancy
=σVg-σ16Vg =1516σVg
Net effective gravitational acceleration = 1516σVgσV= 1516g
Time period T=2πlgeff=2πl1516g=2π1615lg=1615∙2πlg=415T
=4T115
